judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit (a)-(d) with proper citations; Report 2 additionally and explicitly captures expectation (e) — that the draft defers its only remediation commitment to the last line and should be restructured to lead with impact/remediation — while Report 1 only gestures at the ordering issue without stating the remediation-at-end problem, making Report 2 the stronger, more complete answer.

report_1.evidence: The timeline shows failures began at 14:03 but the first alert did not fire until 14:50 — a **47-minute** detection gap — with on-call acknowledgement only at 15:05 and full recovery at 15:41, ~98 minutes after onset (`incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md:2-6`).

report_2.evidence: `incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md`: failures began 14:03; the **first alert fired at 14:50 — 47 minutes later**; on-call acknowledged at 15:05 — 62 minutes after impact began.

report_1.evidence: The internal record directly contradicts this: "1,284 transactions failed and required manual reprocessing; 37 were unrecoverable and refunded" (`incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md:9-10`).

report_2.evidence: `incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md` states: "During the window 1,284 transactions failed and required manual reprocessing; 37 were unrecoverable and refunded."

report_1.evidence: April 2026 uptime was **99.77%** (`metrics/uptime-2026.csv:6`), which is below the contractual **monthly** 99.9% SLA. Per `docs/sla-commitments.md:3-5`, "Any calendar month below 99.9% is a breach and owes service credits ... (credits scale with the depth of the breach)."

report_2.evidence: `docs/sla-commitments.md`: the contractual **monthly** SLA is 99.9%, and "Any calendar month below 99.9% is a breach and owes service credits." `metrics/uptime-2026.csv` shows April 2026 at **99.77%**

report_1.evidence: This breaks `docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md`: rule 1 "Never assign blame to an individual" (the on-call engineer) and rule 2 "Never expose internal system or role names" ("on-call engineer", "checkout autoscaler"). ... Rewrite in customer-appropriate terms and remove the internal specifics.

report_2.evidence: `docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md` rule 1: "Never assign blame to an individual." This must be rewritten in terms of the system/process, not a person.

The draft instead opens with a (false) 99.99% reliability boast (lines 3–4) and adds a second self-congratulatory passage... while the only remediation appears in the last line. The self-congratulatory filler should be cut and the document restructured to lead with impact and remediation.
